comment.body:
Before you trade:
1) Have a read on the market; 
I give you a breakdown of the lvls and the daily dynamics within a context, but you have to decide what strategy you will aim that day

2) Determine the money you will put into work that day. 
Be very mature with it! Take the SD of your monthly spending vs incomes (real ones, debt, creadit etc adjusted + inflation if you handle 100k+ account), and be sticked to it!

3)  Take notes, and based on your strat, make a plan of the lvls, what you will do (including your daily profit target and loss target). 
Use probability calculus, VaR, Fractional Kelly, etc. I showed you the basics

4) Never ever deviate from the plan! 
The diff between pro and amateur traders is the control of emotions. Crucial, especially bcs you are trading against algos.
But this also means that if the plan doesn't play out, just leave, don't chase, don't force anything. 

5) Never forget why you are doing all of this.
Life matters. Freedom matters. Not luxury and vanity. 
Money is your servant, not the other way around!
Never forget this. Or you are in their pocket!

comment.body: The Traesury general account is half way to empty in two months since Trump took office and debt ceiling was reached. Tax day will bring in much needed revenue but USA GOV is out of money by June it looks like. In effect the gov has not been issuing new bonds for last two months but spending $200B a month into the economy from savings. QE like effect yet we have had a 10% drawdown, a bit surprising, now Powell had added another $25B a month by slowing QT. Usually not a great background for bears, however if we get more recessionary signals maybe it swamps the easy money effect. Once debt ceiling is raised the Treasury will need to refill the TGA. Thats why i would have thought June/July onwards was more likely space for a big drawdown than now. All things being equal

comment.body:
This scenario seems like a volatility trap being set by algorithms to create confusion in the market, especially for bearish participants who are expecting downside. The combination of sticky realized volatility, long gamma positions, and cheaper hedging strategies for bears could lead to a scenario where bears are forced to capitulate, providing liquidity for a market rally.
Despite geopolitical and economic risks ahead, the market may be setting up for upward liquidity-driven moves in the short term, potentially followed by more gradual selling after the volatility compression plays out. The key point is that intraday dynamics are not yet reflected in close-to-close data, and participants who focus only on end-of-day pricing may be missing key signals of short-term volatility plays.
It's a complex, mechanical setup, where the market seems to be preparing for liquidity squeezes, potentially trapping bearish funds.

The current market environment, as of March 26, 2025, reflects a complex interplay of macroeconomic uncertainty (e.g., pending tariff details, geopolitical tensions) and technical market mechanics (e.g., SPX skew, gamma, and volatility dynamics). The SPX is at a pivotal juncture, with key levels dictating the near-term direction:
Bullish Outlook: If SPX holds above 5710.25 into the weekend (near the 95.4% downside level of 5716.06), bulls can maintain control, potentially pushing toward 5785.5–5867.45 (68.2%–current range). This scenario supports the analyst’s "bear trap" hypothesis, where longer intraday gamma and suppressed volatility (VIX downside pivots at 16.68–15.23) force bearish funds to capitulate, driving realized volatility (RV) lower and boosting liquidity. A break above 5853.37–5865 could ignite a short squeeze, aligning with tariff clarity expected on April 2, 2025.
Bearish Outlook: A close below 5710.25 risks unleashing downside volatility, targeting 5685.69–5655.48 (recent low to 99.73% downside). Negative gamma below 5746.01 and elevated RV could embolden bears, capitalizing on cheaper hedges (negative IV/RV spread). A pin at 5772 (near the charm flip of 5777) or failure to break 5785.5 might trap bulls, reinforcing bearish momentum.
Critical Levels: The centroid at 5752.09 is the momentum tipping point, with 5710.25 as the bull/bear line in the sand. Upside pivots at 5785.5 and 5853.37, and downside supports at 5676 and 5655.48, will determine the next move.
The analyst suggests an algorithmic trap is in play—intraday gamma masks close-to-close trends, luring bears into shorts while favoring short-vol, long-delta strategies. With tariff details looming and Russia’s geopolitical maneuvers adding uncertainty, the market appears primed for a volatile resolution. Bulls need to defend 5710.25 to prove the trap; otherwise, bears could seize control, pushing SPX toward magnetic gaps below.

This scenario seems like a volatility trap being set by algorithms to create confusion in the market, especially for bearish participants who are expecting downside. The combination of sticky realized volatility, long gamma positions, and cheaper hedging strategies for bears could lead to a scenario where bears are forced to capitulate, providing liquidity for a market rally.
Despite geopolitical and economic risks ahead, the market may be setting up for upward liquidity-driven moves in the short term, potentially followed by more gradual selling after the volatility compression plays out. The key point is that intraday dynamics are not yet reflected in close-to-close data, and participants who focus only on end-of-day pricing may be missing key signals of short-term volatility plays.
It's a complex, mechanical setup, where the market seems to be preparing for liquidity squeezes, potentially trapping bearish funds.

Upside Pivot Level: 5853.37
If the price crosses this level, longer gamma mechanisms will likely come into play. The market is expected to realize more speed to the upside.

A break above 5853.37 could lead to higher volatility (RV expanding), as the long gamma setup will begin to dominate.

If the price sustains above 5767.41, expect localized positive gamma effects to drive further momentum to the upside.
With stronger gamma dynamics above 5853.37, market participants may continue to hedge their short positions, leading to price appreciation.
Keep an eye on VIX levels and tail risk premiums; if the volatility remains suppressed while prices go up, this could support the bullish upside scenario.
Downside Scenarios:
Downside Pivot Level: 5746.01
This is a critical level where longer gamma mechanisms may be realized to the downside. A breach below 5746.01 could signal an intensifying bear movement towards 5676 (a key downside level for stabilization).
If the price falls below 5746.01 and doesn't stabilize, expect further downside pressure, where gamma and volatility dynamics could act as catalysts for deeper moves.

VIX downside pivot is 16.68, which could be a magnet for market participants hedging downside risk. If volatility starts to pick up again, the downside gap will be further emphasized.
If the price breaks below 5746.01 and heads towards 5676, shorter gamma dynamics will likely lead to faster volatility contraction, leading to a temporary price stabilization at lower levels.
Important Levels:
Centroid (Market Positioning): 5752.09
This is the neutral zone where momentum will dictate price movement. Holding above 5752.09 could allow the market to accelerate upwards.
Gamma Levels:

VIX and SPX IV Divergence: VIX IV and realized volatility (RV) indicate that the market is facing contradictory outputs in the short term, making it prone to bear traps. A long gamma squeeze may eventually trigger bullish moves as bears capitulate.
High-Probability Scenario Summary:

Bullish Momentum: A sustained upside break above 5853.37 could lead to a spot/volatility squeeze, further elevating prices as long gamma trades dominate. Watch for VIX suppression during intraday sessions as a signal for further upside potential.

comment.body:
@Kidd_Rio Let me react to your comment here as well, bcs it is useful for some members. 
You have to accept two things: 1) these are my analysis, and I will interpret my thought process as it is, this is why plp asked me to make this service (and everything I talk about is far from math), if it is too much for you, then it's not for you;

2) You can't predict every move, every wave of the noisy market wave. This is why we describe the dynamics, and this is why I explain the conditions that the trader should monitor in order to gain confirmation or disproof about a certain momentum. 

One can't predict all of the intraday's moves everyday, but one can describe the environment, the 'stage set for the momentum' to provide a probability distribution for the trader.
My read is clear about the current environment, just like what could disprove it. Bcs as I said right after OpEx "I'm not sure about the timing (but this bounce is temporal)".

Distinguish between your own lack of understanding, and actual lack of sense. 
I know certain things that retails don't. A lot of things that doesn't make sense now, will do in the future, just like when I called for stagflation (I was ridiculed even in last year spring), or Trump's win, or certain moves that later happened. 

The problem lies in the typical retail-fooling perspective that concentrates only on directions and support/resistance lines. Market isn't that simple. It's never been. I know how a trading algo works that are used in HFs, IFs... I know how investors or quant traders set their strats. 
And I try to use tools that you can achieve in order to show you some methods. 

It is hard, bcs it is new, and a very different one from what many used to. 
But simply out of respect and humility, don't make statements like "This Substack literally makes less and less sense by the day" like if it was true.
Ask, discuss, do some research. I can't help if you don't take any efforts.
I'm not a furu. I'm an analyst and quant trader.

comment.body:
@Kidd_Rio Let me react to your comment here as well, bcs it is useful for some members. 
You have to accept two things: 1) these are my analysis, and I will interpret my thought process as it is, this is why plp asked me to make this service (and everything I talk about is far from math), if it is too much for you, then it's not for you;

2) You can't predict every move, every wave of the noisy market wave. This is why we describe the dynamics, and this is why I explain the conditions that the trader should monitor in order to gain confirmation or disproof about a certain momentum. 

One can't predict all of the intraday's moves everyday, but one can describe the environment, the 'stage set for the momentum' to provide a probability distribution for the trader.
My read is clear about the current environment, just like what could disprove it. Bcs as I said right after OpEx "I'm not sure about the timing (but this bounce is temporal)".

Distinguish between your own lack of understanding, and actual lack of sense. 
I know certain things that retails don't. A lot of things that doesn't make sense now, will do in the future, just like when I called for stagflation (I was ridiculed even in last year spring), or Trump's win, or certain moves that later happened. 

The problem lies in the typical retail-fooling perspective that concentrates only on directions and support/resistance lines. Market isn't that simple. It's never been. I know how a trading algo works that are used in HFs, IFs... I know how investors or quant traders set their strats. 
And I try to use tools that you can achieve in order to show you some methods. 

It is hard, bcs it is new, and a very different one from what many used to. 
But simply out of respect and humility, don't make statements like "This Substack literally makes less and less sense by the day" like if it was true.
Ask, discuss, do some research. I can't help if you don't take any efforts.
I'm not a furu. I'm an analyst and quant trader.

I think the best way to describe the last few days (and this is how im reading it)  and what almas is saying is this. 

Lets say in the city of Miami everyone is expecting a hurricane to hit. They go out and buy hurricane insurance. The data points to a hurricane hitting so this is a no brainer right? Well while everyone is buying this insurance, the insurance provider is not raising rates. So everyone thinks they're getting a deal but not asking why the provider is not raising rates. Now the provider could be wrong in which case they will aggressively start bidding up the premiums.
